Apple___Apple_scab.symptoms: Olive-green to brown spots on leaves and fruit surface. Spots darken with age. Infected leaves may drop early.
Apple___Apple_scab.cause: Fungal — Venturia inaequalis. Spreads via rain splash in cool, wet spring conditions.
Apple___Apple_scab.treatment_organic: Neem oil spray (3ml/L water) every 7 days. Sulfur dust application. Remove and destroy infected leaves.
Apple___Apple_scab.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb 75 WP (2.5 g/L) or Captan 50 WP (2 g/L). Apply at bud break and repeat every 10 days.
Apple___Apple_scab.prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties. Prune for good air circulation. Avoid overhead irrigation. Collect fallen leaves.
Apple___Apple_scab.source: ICAR All India Coordinated Research Project on Fruits

Apple___Black_rot.symptoms: Purple spots on leaves expanding to brown. Fruit shows circular lesions — rotting from core outward. Bark cankers.
Apple___Black_rot.cause: Fungal — Botryosphaeria obtusa. Enters through wounds. Worst in warm, humid conditions.
Apple___Black_rot.treatment_organic: Copper-based fungicide spray. Remove mummified fruits immediately. Prune dead wood.
Apple___Black_rot.treatment_chemical: Captan 50 WP (2 g/L) or Thiophanate-methyl 70 WP (1 g/L). Begin at petal fall.
Apple___Black_rot.prevention: Remove dead branches promptly. Avoid injuries to tree. Sanitize pruning tools.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.symptoms: Yellow-orange spots on upper leaf surface. Tube-like spore structures on leaf undersides.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.cause: Fungal — Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. Requires both apple and cedar/juniper trees to complete life cycle.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.treatment_organic: Neem oil or sulfur sprays during infection periods. Remove nearby juniper trees if possible.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.treatment_chemical: Myclobutanil (Rally 40 WP) or Triadimefon. Apply from tight cluster through 3rd cover sprays.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.prevention: Plant rust-resistant apple cultivars. Remove juniper trees within 1 km radius.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.source: ICAR Annual Report on Integrated Disease Management

Apple___healthy.symptoms: No disease symptoms detected. Leaf appears green and healthy.
Apple___healthy.treatment_organic: Continue regular monitoring. Maintain good soil health with organic matter.
Apple___healthy.prevention: Maintain adequate spacing, nutrition, and irrigation. Monitor regularly.

Blueberry___healthy.prevention: Maintain soil pH 4.5–5.5 for blueberries. Regular pruning.

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves, young shoots, and fruit. Leaves curl and distort. Fruit may crack.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.cause: Fungal — Podosphaera clandestina. Favored by warm days, cool nights, and high humidity without rain.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.treatment_organic: Potassium bicarbonate spray (1 tsp/L). Dilute milk spray (40% milk + 60% water). Neem oil.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.treatment_chemical: Myclobutanil or Trifloxystrobin. Apply at first sign and repeat every 10–14 days.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization. Ensure good air circulation. Water at base, not leaves.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.symptoms: Long, narrow, tan-gray rectangular lesions parallel to leaf veins. Lesions may coalesce causing large blighted areas.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.cause: Fungal — Cercospora zeae-maydis. Spread by wind and rain. Worst in warm, humid conditions.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.treatment_organic: Remove crop debris. Increase plant spacing. Neem oil spray as preventive.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb 75 WP (2.5 g/L) or Azoxystrobin. Apply at first sign, repeat in 14 days.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.prevention: Plant resistant hybrids. Rotate with non-host crops (soybean, groundnut). Reduce crop residue.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.symptoms: Small, circular to elongated, brick-red pustules scattered over both leaf surfaces. Pustules turn dark brown with age.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.cause: Fungal — Puccinia sorghi. Wind-borne spores from distant sources.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.treatment_organic: Neem-based spray preventively. Remove heavily infected leaves.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb + Carbendazim combination. Apply at tassel emergence.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Early planting to avoid peak infection periods.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Long (5–15 cm), cigar-shaped, tan lesions with wavy margins. Starts on lower leaves and progresses upward.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.cause: Fungal — Exserohilum turcicum. Spread by wind and rain splash. Favors cool, wet weather.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.treatment_organic: Remove infected leaves. Apply neem oil as protective spray before tasseling.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.treatment_chemical: Propiconazole 25 EC (1 ml/L) or Mancozeb (2.5 g/L). Apply at first sign.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.prevention: Use resistant hybrids. Crop rotation. Destroy infected debris after harvest.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. Leaves are green and vigorous.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.prevention: Balanced NPK, proper spacing, timely irrigation.

Grape___Black_rot.symptoms: Brown circular spots with dark borders on leaves. Infected berries turn black and shrivel into hard mummies.
Grape___Black_rot.cause: Fungal — Guignardia bidwellii. Spreads rapidly in warm, wet weather. Overwinters in mummified fruit.
Grape___Black_rot.treatment_organic: Remove mummified berries immediately. Copper fungicide spray early season.
Grape___Black_rot.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb or Myclobutanil. Begin applications at bud break every 10–14 days through veraison.
Grape___Black_rot.prevention: Remove all mummified fruit before winter. Good canopy management for air circulation.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).symptoms: Tiger-stripe pattern of yellowing and browning between leaf veins. Berries develop dark spots and may shrivel.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).cause: Fungal complex — Phaeomoniella chlamydospora and others. Enters through pruning wounds.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).treatment_organic: Seal pruning wounds immediately with wound sealant. Remove severely affected wood.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).treatment_chemical: No reliable chemical cure. Preventive: paint pruning cuts with Trichoderma-based biocontrol.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).prevention: Make clean, smooth cuts. Seal all pruning wounds. Avoid pruning in wet weather.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.symptoms: Irregular brown to reddish-brown spots on older leaves. Spots may have yellow borders.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.cause: Fungal — Isariopsis clavispora. Spreads in humid conditions.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.treatment_organic: Copper hydroxide spray. Remove infected leaves. Improve air circulation.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.treatment_chemical: Captan or Mancozeb application at 15-day intervals.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.prevention: Train vines for good air flow. Avoid excessive irrigation on leaves.

Grape___healthy.treatment_organic: Continue balanced nutrition and regular monitoring.
Grape___healthy.prevention: Annual pruning, balanced fertilization, good drainage.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).symptoms: Asymmetric yellowing (blotchy mottle) of leaves. Small, misshapen, bitter fruit. Premature fruit drop. Trees slowly decline.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).cause: Bacterial — Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus. Spread by Asian citrus psyllid insect vector.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).treatment_organic: No cure exists. Remove and destroy infected trees immediately to protect surrounding trees.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).treatment_chemical: Imidacloprid for psyllid control to prevent spread. No systemic treatment available.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).prevention: Use certified disease-free planting material. Control psyllid populations. Quarantine infected zones.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves becoming angular, purple-brown. Leaves yellow and drop. Fruit shows sunken, dark lesions.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.cause: Bacterial — Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni. Spread by rain splash. Worse in warm, wet conditions.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.treatment_organic: Copper-based bactericide spray. Remove infected leaves. Avoid wetting foliage during irrigation.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.treatment_chemical: Oxytetracycline (Mycoshield) or copper hydroxide. Apply from pink bud stage.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid overhead irrigation. Maintain tree vigor through proper nutrition.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Water-soaked, raised spots on leaves, turning brown with yellow halo. Fruit shows raised, scabby lesions.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.cause: Bacterial — Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria. Spread by rain, wind, contaminated tools.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.treatment_organic: Copper hydroxide spray (3 g/L). Remove infected plant material. Avoid handling wet plants.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.treatment_chemical: Copper oxychloride 50 WP (3 g/L). Begin at transplanting and repeat weekly.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Avoid overhead irrigation. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops.

Potato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown-black spots with concentric rings (target-board pattern) on older leaves. Yellow halo around spots.
Potato___Early_blight.cause: Fungal — Alternaria solani. Spread by wind and rain. Worsens with temperature fluctuations.
Potato___Early_blight.treatment_organic: Neem oil spray (5 ml/L). Copper fungicide. Remove infected lower leaves.
Potato___Early_blight.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb 75 WP (2.5 g/L) or Chlorothalonil. Apply at first sign, repeat every 7–10 days.
Potato___Early_blight.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed tubers. Maintain plant nutrition. Adequate plant spacing.

Potato___Late_blight.symptoms: Water-soaked, pale green-brown lesions on leaves. White cottony growth on leaf undersides in humid conditions. Tubers rot.
Potato___Late_blight.cause: Oomycete — Phytophthora infestans. Spreads explosively in cool, wet conditions. Caused Irish Famine 1845.
Potato___Late_blight.treatment_organic: Remove infected plants immediately. Copper fungicide spray. Avoid overhead irrigation.
Potato___Late_blight.treatment_chemical: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) — most effective. Apply preventively in high-risk periods.
Potato___Late_blight.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid irrigating in the evening. Hilling to protect tubers from spores.

Potato___healthy.prevention: Use certified seed potatoes, proper hilling.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaf surface. Leaves turn yellow and die prematurely. Reduces yield significantly.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.cause: Fungal — Podosphaera xanthii. Favored by warm dry days and cool nights. Does not need water to spread.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.treatment_organic: Baking soda spray (1 tbsp/L + few drops dish soap). Dilute milk spray. Neem oil.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.treatment_chemical: Sulfur-based fungicide or Triadimefon. Apply at first sign every 7 days.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid excess nitrogen. Improve air circulation.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.symptoms: Small, purple to reddish-brown spots on upper leaf surface. Centers turn light brown. Severely infected leaves have scorched appearance.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.cause: Fungal — Diplocarpon earlianum. Spreads by rain splash. Worse in wet, humid conditions.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.treatment_organic: Remove infected leaves. Copper-based fungicide spray. Good air circulation.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.treatment_chemical: Captan 50 WP or Myclobutanil. Apply before and after bloom.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove old leaves after harvest. Plant in rows for good air flow.

Strawberry___healthy.prevention: Replace runners every 3 years, good drainage.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves and fruit. Spots turn brown with yellow margins. Fruit shows raised, dark, scabby lesions.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.cause: Bacterial — Xanthomonas spp. Spread by rain splash and infected seed. Worse in warm, wet conditions.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.treatment_organic: Copper hydroxide spray (3 g/L). Remove infected leaves. Avoid wetting foliage.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.treatment_chemical: Copper oxychloride 50 WP (3 g/L) + Mancozeb combination. Apply weekly.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Drip irrigation instead of overhead. Crop rotation.

Tomato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown circular spots with concentric rings on older lower leaves. Yellow halo around lesions. Defoliation from bottom up.
Tomato___Early_blight.cause: Fungal — Alternaria solani. Spreads by wind and rain. Worsens with temperature stress.
Tomato___Early_blight.treatment_organic: Neem oil spray. Remove infected lower leaves. Apply Trichoderma-based biocontrol.
Tomato___Early_blight.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb 75 WP (2.5 g/L) or Chlorothalonil 75 WP. Apply every 7–10 days.
Tomato___Early_blight.prevention: Avoid planting in same field 2 years running. Remove crop debris after harvest. Mulching.

Tomato___Late_blight.symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems turning dark brown. White mold on underside in humid weather. Fruit rots rapidly.
Tomato___Late_blight.cause: Oomycete — Phytophthora infestans. Same pathogen as potato late blight. Spreads very rapidly.
Tomato___Late_blight.treatment_organic: Copper-based spray preventively. Remove infected plants immediately to prevent spread.
Tomato___Late_blight.treatment_chemical: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil) — most effective. Apply at first sign every 7 days.
Tomato___Late_blight.prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation. Do not grow near potato fields. Use resistant varieties.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Pale greenish-yellow spots on upper leaf surface. Olive-green to grayish-brown mold on undersides.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.cause: Fungal — Passalora fulva. Favored by high humidity (>85%). Common in greenhouse tomatoes.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.treatment_organic: Improve ventilation. Reduce humidity. Copper-based spray.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.treatment_chemical: Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil. Apply every 7 days when conditions favor disease.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.prevention: Ensure good air circulation. Avoid overcrowding. Keep humidity below 85%.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Small, circular spots with dark brown borders and lighter centers on lower leaves. Tiny black dots visible in centers.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.cause: Fungal — Septoria lycopersici. Spreads rapidly in warm, wet weather via rain splash.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.treatment_organic: Remove infected leaves immediately. Copper hydroxide spray. Mulch to reduce splash.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.treatment_chemical: Chlorothalonil 75 WP (2 g/L) or Mancozeb. Apply every 7–10 days.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.prevention: Crop rotation. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove crop debris after harvest.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.symptoms: Fine stippling or bronzing of leaves. Fine webbing on underside. Leaves turn yellow and drop. Severe infestation causes defoliation.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.cause: Arachnid — Tetranychus urticae. Not a fungal disease. Thrives in hot, dry conditions. Rapid reproduction.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.treatment_organic: Neem oil spray (5 ml/L) every 5 days. Insecticidal soap spray. Water spray to dislodge mites.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.treatment_chemical: Abamectin 1.9 EC (0.5 ml/L) or Spiromesifen. Rotate chemicals to prevent resistance.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.prevention: Avoid over-application of nitrogen. Maintain adequate moisture. Introduce predatory mites.

Tomato___Target_Spot.symptoms: Brown circular lesions with concentric rings (target pattern) on leaves, stems, and fruit. Fruit lesions become sunken.
Tomato___Target_Spot.cause: Fungal — Corynespora cassiicola. Spread by wind and rain. Favored by warm, humid conditions.
Tomato___Target_Spot.treatment_organic: Neem oil spray. Remove infected leaves. Improve air circulation.
Tomato___Target_Spot.treatment_chemical: Azoxystrobin or Propiconazole. Apply at first detection every 10–14 days.
Tomato___Target_Spot.prevention: Avoid dense planting. Crop rotation. Remove crop debris.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.symptoms: Yellowing and upward curling of leaves. Stunted plant growth. Flowers drop. Severely reduced yield.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.cause: Viral — TYLCV (Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus). Transmitted by whitefly Bemisia tabaci. No cure once infected.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.treatment_organic: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately. Yellow sticky traps for whiteflies. Neem oil to control vector.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.treatment_chemical: Imidacloprid 17.8 SL (0.5 ml/L) for whitefly control. No antiviral treatment available.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Use insect-proof netting. Remove infected plants promptly. Control whitefly population.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.symptoms: Mosaic pattern of light and dark green on leaves. Leaves may appear mottled, puckered, or shoe-string like.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.cause: Viral — ToMV (Tomato Mosaic Virus). Spreads by contact with infected plants, tools, hands. Very stable in soil.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.treatment_organic: Remove infected plants immediately. Wash hands and disinfect tools. Do not smoke near plants (tobacco carries TMV).
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.treatment_chemical: No chemical treatment available. Prevention is the only approach.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.prevention: Use certified virus-free seed. Sanitize all tools. Avoid handling plants after tobacco use.

Tomato___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. Plant appears vigorous and healthy.
Tomato___healthy.treatment_organic: Continue regular monitoring and balanced nutrition.
Tomato___healthy.prevention: Crop rotation, balanced NPK, regular scouting.
